hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. C

assessment.signage: 149 address number visible; storefront unit at centre appears to be a residential-style ground floor unit or small commercial; adjacent light-brick building heavily graffitied on left

photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Facade is occupied and readable from the street, with contemporary storefront alterations layered onto the earlier building form.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Simple fascia and coping with limited decorative trim visible.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney clearly visible from the selected frontage view.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Character comes primarily from surviving massing, cladding, and current storefront treatment.
photo_observations.storefront_description: Compact beauty studio storefront with orange sign band and dark windows.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Ground floor converted to commercial with nail studio signage. Facade painted cream/grey over original brick. Upper floors retain residential window pattern. Original house-form massing clearly legible.
photo_observations.notes: HCD typology is House-form, Row. The original house-form massing is clearly legible despite painted brick and commercial ground floor. Three-storey form with residential upper floors intact.
photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: light cream/grey painted brick with orange commercial sign (nail studio) at ground level
photo_observations.window_details: Upper floors have residential-scale windows in painted brick. Ground floor has commercial storefront glazing and sign.
photo_observations.door_details: Single glazed salon entry integrated into the dark lower facade.
photo_observations.signage_observed: Nail Studio sign and eyebrow thread/lash/mani-pedi pricing posters.

facade_description: 149 Augusta Ave presents a row-house facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, row typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2, 0 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include cornice. HCD note: The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. C
heritage_summary: C. The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles.
